title: Urban Matrimony and the Sandwich Arts

jokes.text: Hot 'n' brown's coffee really wakes me up to the fact that its coffee sucks.
jokes.setup: Jeff mentions being woken up by coffee
jokes.punchline: Subverts expectation - wakes him up to how bad it is
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on 'wakes me up' with misdirection, economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I was woken up by coffee this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Which is ironic, because it also woke me up to how bad it is.

jokes.text: Now where am I going to get my cappuccinos and Sarah McLachlan CDs?
jokes.setup: Lamenting the loss of a coffee shop
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific and dated reference to Sarah McLachlan CDs
jokes.explanation: Perfect for Annie's character - nostalgic and slightly out of touch, specific detail makes it memorable

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: I can't believe they're closing our coffee shop. Where am I supposed to go now?

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: It's like watching a sad Sarah McLachlan commercial, except instead of abused animals, it's my entire social life.

jokes.text: If the good lord wanted you to have a penny, you'd have one.
jokes.punchline: Pierce's twisted logic about divine penny distribution
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Pierce - absurd religious justification for selfishness, memorable phrasing

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: You know what? I think God puts those pennies there. He's like, 'Here, poor people, take a penny.' But then He's also like, 'But I'm gonna make sure there's always one left so you feel like a cheapskate when you don't take it.'

jokes.text: Why do you look like a wealthy murderer?
jokes.punchline: The specific comparison to a wealthy murderer
jokes.explanation: Sharp, specific insult with economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Pierce, you look like a wealthy murderer.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: [Beep] Threat level four. [Beep] Your guests have arrived.
jokes.setup: Pierce demonstrates his security camera device
jokes.punchline: The device's confused threat assessment
jokes.explanation: Visual gag showing malfunctioning technology, sets up Pierce's incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: This is my new security camera system. State-of-the-art threat detection.

dialogue.speaker: Security Camera
dialogue.line: Threat detected. Initiating defensive protocol.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: What? No, I'm just showing you—

dialogue.speaker: Security Camera
dialogue.line: Primary threat: overweight male, questionable fashion sense, holding suspicious device.

jokes.text: Wireless racism. The future of the past is now.
jokes.setup: Pierce's camera malfunctioning on racial recognition
jokes.punchline: Jeff's clever phrase about technological prejudice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant phrase that captures both the absurdity and social commentary, highly quotable

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Even technology can be racist.

jokes.text: I mean, not your people.
jokes.setup: Pierce talking about calling Shirley's people
jokes.punchline: His awkward racial awareness/correction
jokes.explanation: Typical Pierce racial awkwardness, cringe humor

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I'm gonna call Shirley's people.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: My people?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: You know, her family. I'm not being racist.

jokes.text: Baby, I have loved you ever since there was a Soviet union and only one Damon Wayans.
jokes.setup: Andre declaring his love since the early days
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific cultural references to mark time
jokes.explanation: Creative way to establish timeline with specific cultural references

dialogue.speaker: Andre
dialogue.line: I've loved you since the early days. Back when we first met, you were listening to Nelly's 'Hot in Herre' on your Zune.

dialogue.speaker: Andre
dialogue.line: Then you moved on to that phase where you only watched movies on VHS. Remember when you tried to rewind a DVD?

dialogue.speaker: Andre
dialogue.line: And now you're here, in 2013, still the same person I fell for back in the MySpace era.

jokes.text: There's a whole generation of viewers that didn't get to see the original. Let's hope it's more of a bale than a Kilmer situation.
jokes.setup: Second weddings being like movie reboots
jokes.punchline: Comparing wedding quality to Batman actor changes
jokes.explanation: Peak Abed - meta commentary comparing real life to media franchises

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Second weddings are like movie reboots. Nobody asked for them, but here we are.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: The first one was fine. We didn't need a second one. But now we're comparing it to the original and it's never gonna measure up.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: It's like they cast a different Batman. Nobody wanted a new Batman, but now we gotta watch it anyway.

jokes.text: It would go fourth, seventh, second, fifth, first, third, sixth. No, wait... first then fifth. Fourth, seventh, second, first, fifth, third, sixth.
jokes.punchline: The completely arbitrary and changing ranking system
jokes.explanation: Very Troy - nonsensical but delivered with complete confidence

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I rank weddings. Royal weddings are S-tier. Celebrity weddings are A-tier. Friend weddings are B-tier. Family weddings are C-tier. And distant relative weddings are D-tier.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Wait, no. Actually, I'm changing it. Royal weddings are now A-tier because they're too formal. Celebrity weddings are S-tier. But only if they're on TV.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? Friend weddings should be S-tier because you actually care. No wait, that doesn't work because then I went to Jeff's cousin's wedding and that was only C-tier and I cried the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Okay, new system. It's based on the quality of the open bar, the DJ's willingness to play requests, and how many times someone gets divorced at that wedding. It's very scientific.

jokes.text: No, she's just pro-anti.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's succinct description of Britta's contrarian nature
jokes.explanation: Perfect economical phrase that captures Britta's character perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Britta's not anti-wedding. She's anti-fun.

jokes.text: Weddings are like little girls' tea parties, except the women are the stuffed animals, the men are making them talk, and they're not drinking tea, they're drinking antiquated gender roles.
jokes.setup: Britta's feminist critique setup with tea party analogy
jokes.punchline: The extended metaphor ending with 'drinking antiquated gender roles'
jokes.explanation: Very Britta - overwrought feminist analysis delivered earnestly

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: See, this is exactly what I'm talking about. A tea party is just another example of how patriarchal traditions use quaint rituals to reinforce outdated power structures. Women are literally sitting around serving each other tea while drinking antiquated gender roles.

jokes.text: Somebody tell Britta what an analogy is. I know what it is. It's like a thought with another thought's hat on.
jokes.punchline: Britta's hilariously wrong definition of analogy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Britta moment - confidently wrong with a charmingly absurd metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: An analogy is when you compare two things that are... well, they're basically the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That's not an analogy, that's just saying something twice.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: No, see, it's like when you say 'Life is like a box of chocolates' — they're both brown and delicious.

jokes.text: It was invented back when 'till death' meant 'till your first cold.'
jokes.setup: Jeff arguing against marriage as outdated
jokes.punchline: The historical perspective on life expectancy
jokes.explanation: Clever historical observation with good economy

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Marriage is an outdated institution. Historically, people only lived to like 35, so till death do us part meant like a 7-year commitment. Now we're supposed to be with someone for 60 years? That's insane.

jokes.text: I mean, just nut up and die alone.
jokes.punchline: The bluntly delivered dark advice
jokes.explanation: Very Jeff - cynical but delivered with casual brutality

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Look, relationships are just a slow-motion car crash. You're both pretending to enjoy it until the airbags deploy and someone needs a lawyer.

jokes.text: Our church has certain policies regarding things like second weddings, and tight jeans and... Calico cats.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from reasonable to completely arbitrary (calico cats)
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from sensible church rules to absurd specificity

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: The church says no dancing, no drinking, and no interracial couples.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, they're really that strict?

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: And no calico cats.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Calico cats?

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: They're the devil's quilt.

jokes.text: Inspector spacetime ended up marrying himself as a baby.
jokes.setup: Troy and Abed offering wedding advice from their sci-fi show
jokes.punchline: The completely insane plot detail
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd sci-fi reference that sounds plausible for their show

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: When we were doing the sci-fi show, we had this whole thing about alternate dimensions and time travel.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Yeah, and in one episode, my character married his own grandmother to prevent a paradox.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Which created a new timeline where nobody was born.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: But everyone was okay with it because we explained it in a 40-minute bottle episode.

jokes.text: That's the noise people make when they're offended in all the movies that Annie rents.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's specific dig at Annie's movie taste
jokes.explanation: Good character detail about Annie's movie preferences

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Scoff, scoff, scoff.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Annie, your taste in movies is so bad, you think 'The Room' is a masterpiece.

jokes.text: It's, uh, h-t-t-p, colon, forward slash, forward slash, w-w-w, period, members, period, webplicity custom sites... All one word... period, c-o...
jokes.setup: Pierce trying to give a website URL
jokes.punchline: His complete technological incompetence in spelling out a basic URL
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce - old man technology confusion delivered with misplaced confidence

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: It's www dot... excuse me... double-you double-you double-you dot...

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: No wait, it's w w w dot... that's three w's...

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: The website is at... you know what, just ask the Geek Squad.

jokes.text: Ladies and gentlemen, the trouser bench. For the man on the go who makes frequent stops.
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous concept of pants with a built-in bench
jokes.explanation: Absurd invention that perfectly fits Pierce's character and business incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Behold! My latest invention: pants with a built-in bench.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Pierce, why would pants need a built-in bench?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: For when you get tired of standing, Jeffrey. You sit down, and the bench is already there. It's genius.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: That's just... sitting. That's what pants already do.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Not with a BENCH, Britta.

jokes.text: Will somebody please call all the ambulances?
jokes.punchline: His dramatic overreaction requesting multiple ambulances
jokes.explanation: Pierce's typical dramatic overreaction to minor injury

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Call 911! Call 911! We need multiple ambulances!

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: My legs! I can't feel my legs!

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: This is it. This is how it ends. Tell my story.

jokes.text: Yeah, I could go as normal man and you could be my trusty sidekick ordinario.
jokes.setup: Troy trying to be normal for the wedding
jokes.punchline: Creating a superhero concept around being ordinary
jokes.explanation: Troy's inability to be normal even when trying to be normal

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna be normal at this wedding. Completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: You know what? Normal is a superpower. I'm gonna call myself... The Normalizer.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: My power is being so average that criminals get bored and just give up.

jokes.text: We could dye our hair gray. And then we could lay down on the sidewalk and be invisible.
jokes.setup: Planning to dress boringly for the wedding
jokes.punchline: Escalating to complete camouflage and sidewalk lying
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the 'being normal' concept to absurd extremes

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'm just going to wear something boring and blend in.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That's probably a good idea.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'll get some khakis, a beige sweater, maybe a tan blazer.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: That sounds perfect, actually.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Then I'll add some camouflage netting, face paint, and I'll just lie on the sidewalk outside the venue.

jokes.text: My ninth-grade english teacher used to say, 'there will always be a reason not to follow your dreams.' At the time, he meant I was under the age of consent.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that it was actually about inappropriate relationships
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection from inspirational to disturbing, perfect Britta dark past reference

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: My high school teacher once told me, 'The best relationships are the ones where there's a real power imbalance, where one person has complete authority over the other, and the younger person is still figuring out who they are.'

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: He was talking about his relationship with me.

jokes.text: Uh, in the words of my ninth-grade english teacher, 'you are dippin' and dappin' and don't know what's happenin'.'
jokes.setup: Britta mentioned her teacher, now Shirley quotes hers
jokes.punchline: The completely different tone and style of Shirley's teacher
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing class/cultural difference through teacher quotes

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: My teacher used to say, 'You're a disappointment to your family and everyone who knows you.'

jokes.text: Britta laughing hysterically at the idea of planning a wedding
jokes.setup: Britta offered to plan Shirley's wedding
jokes.punchline: Her uncontrollable laughter at the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Perfect character reaction - Britta finding wedding planning hilarious

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I can't do this. I can't plan a wedding. I don't even know what I'm doing with my own life.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Britta, you said you could handle it.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I know what I said, but... *starts laughing uncontrollably* I'm sorry, I'm sorry. It's just... me? Planning a wedding? That's insane. That's the most ridiculous thing anyone's ever asked me to do.

jokes.text: I was serious.
jokes.setup: Everyone assuming Britta was joking about planning the wedding
jokes.punchline: Her genuine offer delivered deadpan after the laughter
jokes.explanation: Good reversal after the laughter beat

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: No, I'm serious. I'll plan the whole thing. I've already started a Pinterest board.

jokes.text: Maybe you're misunderstanding? I mean, from my butt.
jokes.setup: Pierce mentioning parts that need to be extracted
jokes.punchline: His clarification that trouser bench parts are literally stuck in him
jokes.explanation: Crude but in character, callback to earlier invention failure

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I need you to extract some parts that are stuck in me.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Trouser bench parts. They're literally stuck in me.

jokes.text: Meet Shirley, she's modern, she's urban, she's fun, and she's hungry. You probably notice she's black, but not a stereotype... Suburban black.
jokes.setup: Pierce trying to pitch Shirley professionally
jokes.punchline: His completely inappropriate racial categorization
jokes.explanation: Peak Pierce awkwardness around race, painfully in character

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Shirley, I've been thinking about your business potential. You know what you need? A racial demographic breakdown of your customer base.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I'm thinking 40% of your baked goods should appeal to the urban market, 30% to the suburban set, and 30% to... well, let's call them 'the other people.'

jokes.text: Is she suburban or urban? She's half-urban.
jokes.punchline: His attempt to quantify urbanness mathematically
jokes.explanation: Pierce's continued racial confusion with mathematical precision

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I'm 50% black, 50% white, 50% Asian, and 50% Jewish.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: That's 200%.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm very urban.

jokes.text: Her father's a record producer, her mother's a Shirelle.
jokes.setup: Pierce trying to create Shirley's backstory
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific musical reference that makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Pierce's old man music references applied inappropriately

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: So Shirley, before you were a nun, you were in a girl group called The Supremes?

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: No, Pierce. The Supremes were a real group from the '60s.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Right, right. So you were in a group called The Supremes of Greendale?

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: I was never in any girl group, Pierce.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Well you should have been. You've got the voice for it. Like a young Diana Ross meets a middle-aged accountant.

jokes.text: Shirley, Shirley, Shirley, Shirley, Shirley, Shirley, Shirley. Sweet, innocent Shirley. What was the question?
jokes.punchline: Pierce's condescending repetition followed by complete cluelessness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce - patronizing but completely incompetent

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: What are the business projections?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Business... projections. Yes, the business projections. Those are important things to project about the business.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Do you have any actual numbers?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Numbers? Well, there's ones, and twos, and sometimes... threes.

jokes.text: You'd clearly rather be with Halle Berry in 1999.
jokes.setup: Shirley fed up with Pierce's fantasizing
jokes.punchline: Her specific pop culture reference to his distraction
jokes.explanation: Sharp, specific put-down with good cultural reference

jokes.text: I'm not an entrepreneur. I'm just a kid with a bunch of money.
jokes.punchline: His accurate but pathetic self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Rare moment of Pierce self-awareness delivered simply

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I'm not a businessman. I'm a business, man.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: No wait, that's not right. I'm just a man. A man who's bad at business.

jokes.text: Please, please, help me be somebody.
jokes.setup: Pierce admits he's not really an entrepreneur
jokes.punchline: Desperate plea reveals his insecurity
jokes.explanation: Vulnerable Pierce moment that's both touching and awkward. Good character development through humor.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I'm not really an entrepreneur. I just inherited my father's business and made it worse.

jokes.text: Oops. These pockets. Do you know someone that can buy a pen?
jokes.setup: Pierce needed to write something down
jokes.punchline: His inability to handle basic tasks like obtaining a pen
jokes.explanation: Pierce's continued incompetence at basic life skills

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I need to write something down. Does anyone have a pen? A pen? Hello? Why is no one helping me? This is ridiculous. I can't even get a simple pen in this place. How am I supposed to function like this?

jokes.text: We don't call it pink. We don't call anything by its name. That's, like, day-one floral school stuff.
jokes.punchline: The pretentious floral industry naming conventions
jokes.explanation: Good satire of pretentious industry jargon

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: That's pink.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's mauve.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: It's pink.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: It's clearly mauve. The floral industry has very specific naming conventions.

jokes.text: This is citrine. This is opalescent. This is sea glass, cameo, and cerulean.
jokes.punchline: The increasingly elaborate names for basic colors
jokes.explanation: Continues the pretentious color naming theme

dialogue.speaker: Dale
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? That's not blue, that's cerulean.

dialogue.speaker: Dale
dialogue.line: And that's not red, that's vermillion.

dialogue.speaker: Dale
dialogue.line: You can't just call things by their basic color names anymore.

jokes.text: Color me lavender. 'Lavender' means 'impressed.'
jokes.punchline: Her adoption and explanation of the fancy color terminology
jokes.explanation: Annie embracing pretentious terminology, good callback

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Britta, this is beautiful! How did you learn to arrange flowers like this?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Oh, it's all about understanding color theory. See, this is mauve.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Mauve?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: It's like purple, but it has more self-respect.

jokes.text: There are people dying in Uganda.
jokes.punchline: Britta's inappropriate social justice reality check
jokes.explanation: Perfect Britta - inappropriate timing for social consciousness

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Okay, but let's not forget that the wedding industrial complex is a patriarchal construct designed to trap women in cycles of consumer debt and unrealistic beauty standards.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Britta, we're just trying to pick a cake flavor.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Exactly. Cake. A symbol of excess in a world where people are starving.

jokes.text: Troy and Abed being normal.
jokes.setup: They exit the dreamatorium claiming to be normal now
jokes.punchline: Their synchronized, clearly rehearsed 'normal' behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic payoff - being weird while trying to be normal

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: We're normal now.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Just two regular guys.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Doing normal things.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: In a normal way.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Yes. Normal.

jokes.text: You once convinced Troy that turtlenecks were made of turtles' necks.
jokes.setup: Annie commenting on Jeff's persuasive abilities
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous example of Jeff manipulating Troy
jokes.explanation: Great character detail showing both Jeff's manipulation and Troy's gullibility

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Jeff could convince Troy that he's not actually Troy.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Troy, you're not actually Troy.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: What? Yes I am!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Are you though?

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I... I think so?

jokes.text: Keeping turtlenecks as an option for myself, down the road.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's honest admission of his selfish motivation
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Jeff's self-aware manipulation

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: You're just manipulating everyone to get what you want!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You're right. I am. Because I'm a terrible person and I don't care about any of you.

jokes.text: Where'd I get all that money I'm holding?
jokes.punchline: His complete focus on the wrong details
jokes.explanation: Pierce missing the point of his own presentation

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Gentlemen, as you can see from this pie chart, the demographic breakdown is represented in a series of colorful segments. Notice how the blue represents 34 percent, the red represents 28 percent, and the green — well, the green is my favorite color, so I made that one slightly bigger even though it's only 23 percent.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Also, I had this printed on cardstock instead of regular paper because cardstock feels more professional. You can tell by running your fingers across it. Go ahead, touch it.

jokes.text: I refused to give Santa a christmas list, because I didn't want to depend on any man for anything.
jokes.punchline: Her childhood feminist stance against Santa Claus
jokes.explanation: Perfect Britta - taking feminism to absurd childhood extremes

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: My parents raised me to question authority from an early age. So when I was six, I realized Santa Claus was basically a tool of the patriarchy used to control children's behavior through surveillance and reward systems.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I wrote him a letter explaining how his entire business model was morally corrupt and sexist.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: My parents were so proud. They framed it.

jokes.text: I'm Betty Crocker. I'm Martha Stewart. I'm one of the Steppenwolf wives.
jokes.setup: Britta realizing she's good at domestic tasks
jokes.punchline: The escalation from real people to fictional literary reference
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with literary reference showing Britta's education

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I'm actually good at this! I can cook, I can clean, I can organize...

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I'm like a 1950s housewife!

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I'm like Martha Stewart!

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I'm like... Hazel from Watership Down.

jokes.text: We're heavily invested in your situation.
jokes.setup: Troy and Abed acting 'normal' at the rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Their overly formal, robotic concern
jokes.explanation: Their attempt at normal emotion comes across as robotic

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Abed, I am experiencing what humans call 'concern' for your well-being.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Troy, I reciprocate this emotion. Your health parameters are within acceptable ranges.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I have calculated that friendship involves asking about your day. How was your day?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: My day was adequate. I processed approximately 847 social interactions. You?

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I also did the things. Normal things that normal people do.

jokes.text: 'Webster's dictionary defines'? That's the Jim Belushi of speech openings... It accomplishes nothing, but everyone keeps using it and nobody understands why.
jokes.punchline: Annie's specific comparison to Jim Belushi's career
jokes.explanation: Brilliant analogy - specific, accurate, and unexpectedly harsh. Peak Annie wit.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'd like to propose a toast...

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Your career trajectory is like Jim Belushi's, except people actually wanted you to succeed.

jokes.text: That took me eight hours and six macallan neatses.
jokes.punchline: The excessive time and alcohol required for such a cliche
jokes.explanation: Shows Jeff's drinking and perfectionism leading to mediocrity

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'd like to propose a toast. To friendship. And to the bonds we share.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Bonds that transcend the mundane, the ordinary, the everyday.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Bonds that are... well, they're beautiful, really.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: And that's why I've been drinking since 9 AM.

jokes.text: And how fiscal will the quarterly earnings be?
jokes.punchline: His nonsensical business jargon question
jokes.explanation: Pierce trying to sound professional with meaningless business speak

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: We need to synergize our core competencies and leverage our bandwidth to create a paradigm shift in the marketplace.

jokes.text: Don't you dare use your sexy voice on me.
jokes.punchline: Andre's awareness and resistance to her manipulation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing relationship dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Andre, I'm so sorry. I didn't mean to hurt you.

dialogue.speaker: Andre
dialogue.line: Shirley, I know exactly what you're doing. You apologize, I forgive you, and then next week we're back here again.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: That's not fair.

dialogue.speaker: Andre
dialogue.line: Fair? You've been using 'I'm sorry' like a get-out-of-jail-free card since we got married. Well, the card's expired.

jokes.text: These shrimp are to die for.
jokes.punchline: Pierce's completely inappropriate food comment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce timing - oblivious to serious moment

jokes.text: What if once you go from being weird to normal, you can never go back to being weird again?
jokes.setup: Abed reflecting on their normalization process
jokes.punchline: The existential fear about losing their essential weirdness
jokes.explanation: Meta commentary on character identity and change

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I've been getting better at social interactions. Blending in. Being normal.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: But here's what terrifies me: what if I get too good at it? What if one day I wake up and I'm just... regular? That the weird part of me that made me, me, is just... gone?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'd have successfully assimilated into normal society, but at what cost? I'd be socially functional and completely unrecognizable to myself.

jokes.text: Shut up, Leonard! Those teenage girls you play ping-pong with are doing it ironically.
jokes.punchline: Jeff's specific and harsh comeback about Leonard's social life
jokes.explanation: Classic 'Shut up Leonard' with specific personal attack

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Jeff, can I say something?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: No. Leonard, you don't get to talk. You've forfeited that right by being alive and in this room.

jokes.text: My daddy said he would stay with my mommy forever, and he left! Marriage is a lie!
jokes.punchline: His personal trauma spilling out inappropriately
jokes.explanation: Raw character moment showing Jeff's deep issues

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I just want to say, marriage is... it's a beautiful thing. It's a commitment to another person, and I respect that, because I've never been able to commit to anything in my life. My father left when I was young, and my mother... well, she tried her best, but she was always more interested in her wine collection than in me. So here I am, a grown man who can't maintain a relationship for more than a few months because I'm terrified of abandonment.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: But you two... you two are different. You're going to make it work. You're going to be happy together, unlike my parents, who should have never gotten married in the first place. My dad was a philanderer and my mom was an enabler, and I've spent my entire adult life trying to compensate for their failures by being superficially charming and emotionally unavailable.

jokes.text: I'm gonna stay with somebody forever. It's in my stupid DNA. I was born to be a wife and that's all I'm ever gonna be!
jokes.setup: Britta's feminist stance versus wedding planning success
jokes.punchline: Her drunken admission of traditional desires
jokes.explanation: Britta's core contradiction exposed through alcohol

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I can't believe I'm actually good at planning weddings. I'm supposed to be above all this patriarchal nonsense.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: But honestly? *takes drink* I just want a big white dress and a man to tell me what to do.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Wait, did I say that out loud?

jokes.text: I promise to earn no more than 70% of what you would make at the same job.
jokes.setup: Mock wedding vows between Jeff and Britta
jokes.punchline: Her incorporation of wage gap statistics into vows
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Britta - mixing social justice with romantic commitment

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I promise to love you, to cherish you, and to constantly remind you that women earn 77 cents for every dollar a man makes, which means I'm basically getting married at a 23% discount.

jokes.text: Pick a number, dick!
jokes.punchline: Britta's hostile response with gendered insult
jokes.explanation: Britta's aggressive feminism in personal moment

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Oh, so now you want to talk about babies? That's rich coming from you. You know what your problem is, Jeff? You're like a baby yourself—except babies eventually grow up and stop being selfish little man-children.

jokes.text: It's a risk that you take together. And you don't just promise it once. You got to promise it every day.
jokes.punchline: Surprisingly mature wisdom from Troy of all people
jokes.explanation: Subverts expectations - Troy being genuinely wise

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: You know what? Sometimes the most important thing in a relationship is just... being there. Not trying to fix everything, not making it about you. Just being present.

jokes.text: iPods aren't going away, and maybe it's time to let somebody else take the lead?
jokes.punchline: Shirley's gentle but firm reality check about technology
jokes.explanation: Good character moment of practical Shirley

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Andre, honey, I love you, but that stereo business isn't going to work. Technology moves too fast. You're trying to sell equipment that's already obsolete.

jokes.text: You can save like, um, 70 bucks.
jokes.setup: Offering to marry Shirley and Andre right now
jokes.punchline: The modest financial incentive for immediate marriage
jokes.explanation: Practical Dean reducing romance to economics

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I can marry you right now. I'm a minister.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Well, I got my license online for $15. But the point is, I can do it. And if you do it today, I'll throw in a $50 gift certificate to the cafeteria.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: That's a $65 value.

jokes.text: Lame.
jokes.punchline: Their synchronized dismissal of genuine emotion
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan dismissal of romance while being 'normal'

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: This is beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: It's formulaic.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Yeah, but it works.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Only because we're trained to respond to it.

jokes.text: What'll they think of next?
jokes.setup: Troy showing him a basic dance move
jokes.punchline: Abed's overly amazed reaction to mundane things while being 'normal'
jokes.explanation: Abed's version of normal amazement at basic things

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God! That's... that's incredible, Troy. The way your hips moved through space and time. I've never seen anything like it. Is this what normal people do? Just... move like that? Casually? Without training?

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: It's just a basic step-touch.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: A basic... step-touch. Of course. I should have known. How do you even function in society with this kind of power?

jokes.text: What sort of creature would do a dance called the Lindbergh lean? Blorgons?
jokes.setup: Abed slipping back into Inspector Spacetime mode
jokes.punchline: Their return to sci-fi fantasy language and concepts
jokes.explanation: Perfect return to their weird dynamic through sci-fi framework

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: The temporal rift has collapsed. We must realign the dimensional constants.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Inspector Spacetime protocol. We need to recalibrate the chronometer before the paradox spreads.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Dude, we're just trying to get pizza.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: The pizza exists in multiple dimensions simultaneously until observed. Schrödinger's marinara.

jokes.text: I'm a blast at weddings.
jokes.setup: Dean just ruined Shirley's business dreams
jokes.punchline: His oblivious assessment of his social skills
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dean obliviousness to social damage

dialogue.speaker: Dean
dialogue.line: You know, I'm actually quite good with people. I have a very high emotional intelligence.

jokes.text: Remember that temper tantrum he threw when Adam Sandler 'stole' his idea for Jack and Jill?
jokes.punchline: His delusional belief that he created a terrible movie concept
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce delusion with specific pop culture reference

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Pierce actually thinks he came up with that terrible movie idea.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Of course he does. The man lives in a constant state of delusion.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: I tried to tell him it was based on something he saw, but he wouldn't listen.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: It's my idea! I'm a creative genius!

jokes.text: Look at me now, dad! How's it taste? How many sandwich places did you have? None!
jokes.punchline: His immediate childish tantrum directed at his dead father
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce - immediately proving everyone's point about his immaturity

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: You think I'm immature? Well, I'll show you mature! Dad, wherever you are, you always said I'd never amount to anything, and you know what? You were right! I hope you're happy in hell!

jokes.text: How many sandwich places did you have? [Cackles] None!
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's celebrating having zero sandwich places
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper to Pierce's delusion - celebrating having literally nothing. The cackle sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I'm celebrating my victory!

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Zero sandwich places.

tmdb_overview: When Shirley's remarriage to Andre threatens to put her dreams of starting a business with Pierce on hold, Britta steps in.
insight: Wedding chaos generates 68 jokes at 2.68-per-minute clip, anchored by character-driven cringe.
